title: Calculate Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)
description: Compute the mean absolute deviation by finding the mean, calculating each data point’s absolute deviation from the mean, and averaging those deviations.
condition: Given a finite numerical data set.
response: 1) Compute the mean. 2) Find each absolute deviation |x - mean|. 3) Compute the mean of those deviations.
label: Principle

title: Classify Categorical Data
description: Determine that a data set is categorical when it consists of names, labels, or group categories rather than measurements.
condition: A data set consists of items, names, labels, or non-numeric groupings (e.g., grocery items).
response: Classify the data set as categorical.
label: Concept
example: Data: {milk, bread, apples}. These are item names, so the data are **categorical**.
notes: Applies when numbers are not used to measure quantity.

title: Classify Numerical Data
description: Determine that a data set is numerical when it consists of counts or measurements represented by numbers.
condition: A data set consists of measurable quantities or counts (e.g., total cost).
response: Classify the data set as numerical.
label: Concept
example: Data: Total grocery cost = $42.75. This is a numerical measurement, so the data are **numerical**.
notes: Includes integers, decimals, and other quantitative measures.

title: Compare Means to Assess Center
description: Determine which distribution has a greater typical value by comparing their means.
condition: Given two distributions with different mean values.
response: Identify the distribution with the larger mean as having the greater center.
label: Principle / Model
example: Plant A mean = 51.2 and Plant B mean = 50.1. Since $51.2 > 50.1$, Plant A has the greater mean weight.

title: Compare Medians from Box Plots
description: Compare the medians of two distributions shown in box plots to determine which has the higher typical value.
condition: Given two box plots with marked median lines.
response: Identify and compare the median values to determine which group has a higher or similar typical value.
label: Principle / Model
example: If both box plots show a median at $60$ (thousand dollars), then the typical salaries are the same.

title: Compare Spread Using MAD
description: Determine which data set has greater variability by comparing their MAD values.
condition: Given two data sets with their MADs calculated.
response: Identify the data set with the larger MAD as having greater spread.
label: Principle
example: If MAD(A)=3 and MAD(B)=5, then data set B has the greater spread.

title: Compare Variability Using IQR
description: Compare the interquartile ranges of two distributions to determine which has more variability in the middle 50% of the data.
condition: Given two box plots or two IQR values for different data sets.
response: Identify which IQR is larger and conclude that the distribution with the larger IQR has greater variability in the middle 50%.
label: Principle / Model
example: If Company A has IQR $= 40$ and Company B has IQR $= 70$, then Company B has more variability in the middle 50% of salaries.

title: Compute Mean Minus MAD
description: Subtract the MAD from the mean to estimate a typical lower value in a distribution.
condition: Given a mean and a MAD for a distribution.
response: Compute mean − MAD.
label: Principle / Model

title: Construct Histogram from Data
description: Create a histogram by grouping numerical data into intervals (bins) and representing frequencies with bar heights.
condition: Given a list of numerical data values.
response: Choose intervals, tally frequencies in each interval, and draw bars with corresponding heights.
label: Principle
example: For data {5,10,15,25}, using bins 0–10, 10–20, 20–30 gives frequencies 1,2,1.
notes: Intervals may vary; any correct binning acceptable.

title: Count Data in Histogram Interval
description: Determine how many data points fall within a specified histogram interval by reading bar heights.
condition: Given a histogram and a specified interval (e.g., greater than 20 minutes).
response: Sum the frequencies of the bars that correspond to the specified interval.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the bars for 20–22 minutes and 22–24 minutes have heights 3 and 3, then the total waiting longer than 20 minutes is $3 + 3 = 6$.

title: Describe Even Distribution Shape
description: Recognize and describe when data values are fairly evenly distributed across a range.
condition: Given a dot plot where frequencies across values are relatively similar.
response: State that the distribution is fairly even or uniformly distributed across the range.
label: Concept
example: If dot counts from 2 to 12 are similar in height, describe the distribution as fairly evenly distributed between 2 and 12.

title: Find Missing Value Given Mean
description: Use the definition of mean to determine an unknown data value.
condition: Given the mean of n numbers and n−1 known values.
response: Compute total sum = mean × n; subtract sum of known values to find missing value.
label: Principle
example: Mean of 4 numbers is 40 ⇒ total = 160. Known sum = 35+41+41=117. Missing value = 160−117=43.

title: Identify Center by Balancing Points
description: Estimate the center of a distribution by identifying a value with roughly equal data on both sides.
condition: Given a dot plot or distribution where data appear balanced around a value.
response: State the approximate center as the value with about equal numbers of points on either side.
label: Principle / Model
example: If there are about the same number of dots on either side of 7, identify 7 as the center.

title: Identify Range of Distribution
description: Determine the spread of a distribution by identifying its minimum and maximum values.
condition: Given a dot plot with visible smallest and largest data values.
response: State the distribution extends from the minimum value to the maximum value.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the smallest value shown is 2 and the largest is 12, state the data range from 2 to 12.

title: Identify Statistical Question
description: Recognize whether a question anticipates variability in data and requires collecting data to answer.
condition: Given a question prompt.
response: Classify it as statistical if it anticipates variability and requires data; otherwise classify it as non-statistical.
label: Principle
example: "Which spoon do bakers use most?" is statistical because responses vary across individuals.

title: Identify Typical Value from Histogram Peak
description: Estimate a typical value by identifying the interval with the greatest frequency and selecting a representative value within it.
condition: Given a histogram with one interval having the highest bar.
response: Select a reasonable representative value (often the midpoint) of the tallest interval as the typical value.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the 6–10 minute interval has the tallest bar, choose a typical value such as $8$ minutes.

title: Identify Unusual Value from Plot
description: Detect a data point that is far from the rest of the cluster in a dot plot.
condition: Given a visual dot plot of numerical data.
response: State the value that lies noticeably distant from the main cluster as unusual.
label: Production / Skill
example: If most values are near 500 mg and one point is at 1000 mg, then 1000 mg is unusual.

title: Interpret Dot Plot Counts and Range
description: Extract numerical information such as counts, minimum, maximum, and range from a dot plot.
condition: Given a dot plot displaying discrete data values.
response: Count dots at each value; determine minimum, maximum, and compute range if needed.
label: Production / Skill
example: If smallest value is 1 and largest is 7, range = 7−1 = 6.

title: Interpret Histogram Total and Participation
description: Determine total number of data points by summing frequencies across all histogram bins.
condition: Given a histogram with bar heights representing frequencies.
response: Add frequencies of all bins to find total count.
label: Principle
example: If bin frequencies are 9,7,5,4,3, total = 9+7+5+4+3 = 28 people.

title: Interpret IQR in Context
description: Interpret the interquartile range as the spread of the middle 50% of data in a real-world context.
condition: Given a numerical IQR and a contextual description of the data (e.g., rent, salary).
response: State that the middle 50% of values fall within that range and describe what that spread means in context.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the IQR is $\$850$, then the middle 50% of rents differ by $\$850$, meaning typical rents vary within that amount.

title: Interpret MAD as Variability
description: Understand that the mean absolute deviation represents the typical distance of data values from the mean.
condition: Given the MAD of a distribution.
response: Explain that a larger MAD means more spread and a smaller MAD means more consistency.
label: Rule / Plan
example: A MAD of $1.8$ pounds means bag weights typically differ from the mean by about $1.8$ pounds.

title: Interpret Mean as Typical Value
description: Understand that the mean represents the typical or average value of a distribution.
condition: Given the mean of a distribution in context.
response: State that the mean represents the typical value for the data set.
label: Rule / Plan
example: A mean of $50.1$ pounds indicates the typical bag weight is about $50.1$ pounds.

title: Interpret Median in Context
description: Interpret the median as the value that splits a data set into two equal halves in a real-world situation.
condition: Given a numerical median and a contextual description of the data.
response: State that half the data values are at or above the median and half are at or below, and explain what that implies in context.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the median rent is $\$2000$, then half of apartments cost at least $\$2000$ and half cost $\$2000$ or less.

title: Interpret Range from Box Plot
description: Use the minimum and maximum values shown in a box plot to describe the overall spread (range) of a distribution.
condition: Given a box plot displaying minimum and maximum values.
response: Determine the approximate range (max − min) and interpret what it indicates about overall spread.
label: Principle / Model
example: If the minimum salary is $20$ and the maximum is $270$ (thousand dollars), then the range is about $250$ thousand dollars.

title: Recognize Identifier Numbers as Categorical
description: Determine that numbers used only as labels or identifiers (not measurements) represent categorical data.
condition: Numbers are used as codes or identifiers (e.g., barcode numbers) rather than quantities.
response: Classify the data as categorical.
label: Concept
example: Barcode: 0123456789. The number identifies a product and does not measure anything, so it is **categorical** data.
notes: Students may misclassify because the data contain digits.

title: Use Mean and MAD to Assess Risk of Falling Below a Threshold
description: Use the mean and variability (MAD) together to judge whether values may reasonably fall below a given benchmark.
condition: Given a mean, a MAD, and a threshold value for comparison.
response: Reason that if mean − MAD is below the threshold, values below the threshold are plausible or common.
label: Rule / Plan
example: Plant A: $51.2 - 1.8 = 49.4$, which is below $50$, so it is plausible that many bags weigh under $50$ pounds.
notes: Heuristic reasoning using variability; not a guaranteed bound.
